title: The Effects of Pensions and Retirement Policies on Retirement in Higher Education
context: This paper studies retirement decisions of tenured male arts-and-sciences faculty at 26 high-quality private U.S. colleges (COFHE) using employment observed as of December 31, 1975 and retirements during 1973–78, with pension plan details reported in 1979 (and 1989 for simulations). Institutional variation includes mandatory retirement ages (65–70), normal and early-retirement eligibility, pension accruals and early-retirement supplements, and Social Security rules. The authors estimate a structural life-cycle retirement model and use Monte Carlo simulations to predict effects of raising/eliminating mandatory retirement and offering early-retirement incentives.

title: Is Inequality Harmful for Growth? Theory and Evidence
context: Combining a politico‑economic growth model with empirical tests, the paper examines whether initial income inequality reduces subsequent per‑capita GDP growth. It uses two complementary data sets: a historical panel of nine now‑developed countries divided into 20‑year episodes covering primarily 1830–1970, and a post‑war cross‑section of about 67 countries over 1960–85. Units are country (or country‑period) observations; key covariates are pre‑period inequality (top‑20% share or top20/bottom40 ratio), schooling, franchise restrictions, and initial GDP per capita, exploiting cross‑country and over‑time variation in institutions and redistribution.

title: Do Gasoline Prices Respond Asymmetrically to Crude Oil Price Changes?
context: This empirical study examines asymmetric transmission of crude oil price changes to gasoline prices using weekly and semi‑monthly data from January 1986–December 1990. title: On the Nature of Capital Adjustment Costs
context: This paper analyzes a balanced panel of about 7,000 U.S. manufacturing plants continuously operating from 1972–1988 (Longitudinal Research Database). title: The Effects of Urban Concentration on Economic Growth
context: This paper uses a cross-country panel of about 80–100 countries over 1960–1995 (five-year intervals) to estimate how national urban concentration—measured by primacy (the largest metro area’s share of national urban population)—affects economic growth. title: What Do Parents Value in Education? An Empirical Investigation of Parents' Revealed Preferences for Teachers
context: This paper uses administrative and survey data from a mid-size public school district in the western United States to analyze parents' revealed preferences for elementary school teachers. It studies parental teacher requests collected in the 2003–04 and 2004–05 school years (with a principal survey in February 2003), covering 11 of 13 elementary schools and 251 teacher-year observations. title: PPP Strikes Back: Aggregation and the Real Exchange Rate
context: This empirical study uses monthly Eurostat sectoral CPI price indices (19 goods across ≈13 European countries; about 222 country–sector series) and aggregate CPI real exchange rates versus the US dollar over roughly 1975–1996. title: What's News in Business Cycles
context: This paper estimates a structural DSGE model for the postwar U.S. economy using quarterly data from 1955:Q1–2006:Q4. title: Ambiguous Business Cycles
context: This paper estimates a medium-scale DSGE model for the United States using quarterly macro data (1984Q1–2010Q1) to quantify how time-varying ambiguity (agents’ changing confidence about productivity) drives business cycles. title: Wages, Profits and Rent-Sharing
context: This paper analyzes US manufacturing wage determination using CPS March microdata for roughly 400,000 workers aggregated into 19 industry-by-year cells over 1964–1985. Working from a menu‑cost model of nominal rigidity, the authors argue that skewness in relative‑price changes (for example, large oil shocks) shifts the short‑run Phillips curve. Empirically they compute yearly moments (weighted and unweighted) of industry price changes, estimate AR(1) time‑series and Phillips‑curve regressions including detrended unemployment, and examine historical episodes.

title: Short and Long Run Externalities
context: Using U.S. manufacturing data for 450 four-digit industries from 1958–1986 (with a 1977 input–output matrix to construct interindustry weights), the paper analyzes how external economies transmit through customer (output-weighted) and supplier (input-weighted) linkages. Units are four-digit manufacturing industries; the study distinguishes short-run (fluctuation-oriented) effects from longer-run (growth-oriented) effects and conducts robustness checks for capacity utilization and alternative activity measures (value added vs. gross production). Identification exploits variation across industries and over time in the postwar U.S. manufacturing sector.

title: The Welfare Effects of Encouraging Rural-Urban Migration
context: This paper studies rural–urban migration in northern Bangladesh using randomized migration-subsidy experiments conducted in the Rangpur region (notably a 2008 RCT with follow-ups and a larger 2014 replication). title: Globalization, Trade Imbalances and Labor Market Adjustment
context: A quantitative study of globalization’s labor-market effects using a structural multi-country, multi-sector general equilibrium model estimated to 2000 cross‑sectional and time‑series moments. title: Changes in Social Network Structure in Response to Exposure to Formal Credit Markets
context: This paper studies how microfinance entry changed household social, informational, and financial networks in India. Laws governing IPO prospectuses and enforcement in each country (legal rules as of Dec 2000) are coded, and market outcomes use five-year averages (1996–2000) of capitalization, number of listed firms, and IPO activity; country is the unit of observation. The setting contrasts private-enforcement features (disclosure requirements, burden of proof) and public enforcement (regulators’ powers), and exploits legal-origin variation as an instrument to address endogeneity.

title: Benchmark Interest Rates When the Government is Risky
context: This paper analyzes U.S. fixed-income markets, using monthly asset data (Treasury yields, OIS, IRS/LIBOR swaps, and U.S. sovereign CDS) from roughly 2002–2018 and macro series (consumption, output, government spending, inflation; macro sample back to 1982). title: Common Risk Factors in Currency Markets
context: This paper analyzes monthly spot and one-month forward exchange rates for 37 developed and emerging currencies from November 1983 to March 2008, from the perspective of a U.S. investor. It forms six portfolios sorted by forward discounts (interest differentials), accounts for bid–ask transaction costs, and links cross-sectional carry-trade returns and time-series predictability to a common global stochastic discount factor. The analysis situates currency risk premia in a no-arbitrage affine asset-pricing framework and relates them to U.S. macrofinancial indicators (industrial production, payrolls, VIX).

It contrasts US, global financial-sector, and domestic factors, exploiting cross-sectional variation in banking exposures, trade and financial openness, macro fundamentals (e.g., current account, budget balance, political risk), and crisis-era banking policies (debt/deposit guarantees, capital injections) to explain heterogeneity in contagion.

title: Real Effects of Supplying Safe Private Money
context: The paper studies how introducing federally regulated “national” bank notes affected local economic development in the United States in the late 19th century (primarily changes from ~1875–1885 and outcomes through 1890, with dynamics to 1900). The unit of analysis is towns/counties (town populations and county-level agricultural and manufacturing outcomes). title: Making the House a Home: The Stimulative Effect of Home Purchases on Consumption and Investment
context: The paper analyzes U.S. homeowners and residential properties primarily over 2001–2013 (permit data 1994–2013) using monthly household panel data from the Consumer Expenditure Survey and property-level BuildFax permit records for ~9 million homes. title: The Reserve Supply Channel of Unconventional Monetary Policy
context: This paper studies how large Federal Reserve reserve injections from QE (primarily 2007–2018, focusing on the 2008–2017 QE episode) affected US commercial banks’ balance sheets and loan supply. title: Hostility in Takeovers:  In the Eyes of the Beholder?
context: This paper empirically analyzes 2,346 takeover offers for U.S. exchange-listed target firms from 1975–1996. Units are takeover offers and the associated target and bidder firms; outcomes include deal success, auctions, premiums (runup and markup) and accounting performance. Hostility is measured via WSJ/DJNR and SDC classifications, unnegotiated tender offers, and pre-bid 13D/rumor activity. context: This paper studies how the COVID-19 pandemic (primarily 2020, comparing Dec 2019–Dec 2020 against pre-2014–2019 averages) reshaped within‑metropolitan housing values across the largest 30 U.S. MSAs. title: Why do firms hold so much cash? A tax-based explanation
context: This paper studies U.S. multinational corporations using Compustat panel data from 1982–2004 and detailed BEA affiliate benchmark data (1982, 1989, 1994, 1999). The units are U.S. parent firms and their foreign affiliates. title: Financial Inclusion, Economic Development, and Inequality: Evidence from Brazil
context: The paper evaluates Brazil’s 2004 ‘Banks for All’ (Banco para Todos) program using administrative employer–employee and bank-branch data from 2000–2014. title: Global Growth Opportunities and Market Integration

title: Real and Private-Value Assets
context: This paper synthesizes empirical analyses of real and private-value assets across historical and contemporary settings (long-run samples from the 19th–20th centuries through decades up to 2020), focused primarily on the U.S. but also drawing evidence from the U.K., France, Netherlands, and Singapore. title: 'Lucas' In The Laboratory
context: Laboratory asset-market experiments run in multiple sessions at Caltech, UCLA and the University of Utah, with human subjects trading two long‑lived assets (a risky “Tree” with i.i.d. High/Low dividends and a consol Bond) under a controlled infinite‑horizon design (random termination, continuation probability β=5/6). title: Decision Fatigue and Heuristic Analyst Forecasts
context: This paper studies sell-side analyst forecasting in U.S. equity markets using I/B/E/S forecast records for 2002–2015. The authors estimate four-state Markov-switching models on daily value-weighted CRSP market returns from 1967 Q3–2017 Q2, and calibrate state-dependent transaction-cost multipliers using 81,744 large-order executions (VWAP and PoV) on S&P 500 stocks from Jan 2011–Dec 2012. Units are daily market returns and dollar orders; the setting is U.S. equity markets with time-varying volatility, liquidity regimes, and institutional trading algorithms, used to evaluate in- and out-of-sample trading strategies.

title: Employee Sentiment and Stock Option Compensation
context: Analyzes US publicly traded firms (unbalanced panel from ExecuComp/CRSP/Compustat) over 1992–2003 to study non-executive employee stock option grants at the firm-year level. title: Volatility in an Era of Reduced Uncertainty: Lessons from Pax Britannica
context: This study uses monthly British 3% consol prices (end-of-month, Sept 1729–Dec 1959) to analyze changes in bond-market volatility across historical regimes. The unit is monthly consol returns for the UK, with comparisons to French 3% rentes. Key episodes include the Napoleonic Wars (1803–1815), Pax Britannica (1816–1913), the classical gold-standard era (1880–1913), WWI and WWII. The analysis controls for inflation, recessions, a callability premium and applies structural-break tests to link political/institutional stability to reduced financial volatility.

title: Uncertainty and Economic Activity: A Multi-Country Perspective
context: This paper analyzes quarterly country-level data on real GDP growth and realized equity-market volatility for 32 advanced and emerging economies (covering >90% of world GDP) over 1993:Q1–2011:Q2. Units are country-quarter observations. title: Executive Pay and Performance: Evidence from the U.S. Banking Industry
context: This paper analyzes U.S. commercial banks in the 1980s (roughly 1980–1989/1981–1990) using a panel of 147 publicly traded banks across 36 states and DC. title: Macroeconomic Conditions and Capital Raising
context: This empirical paper studies how U.S. macroeconomic conditions affect firms’ capital raising using issue-level data on 21,657 public bond issues, 7,746 seasoned equity offerings, 40,097 syndicated loans and 12,048 private placements across 1971–2007 (loans from 1988, placements from 1981). context: Using U.S. firm-year data (primarily S&P 500 non‑financial firms) over 1960–2014 (R&D data from 1972 onward), the paper operationalizes aggregate efficiency as the cross‑sectional variance of the manager’s conditional expectation of future cash flows.
